nativeRange.habitat: Coral-rich lagoons and seaward reefs; depth range 0–30 m

diet.notes: Primarily feeds on macroalgae and microalgae (nori, caulerpa, chaetomorpha, hair algae, bubble algae). Benefits from dried seaweed sheets clipped in the tank. Supplement with herbivore pellets and flakes. May occasionally accept meaty foods such as mysis or brine shrimp. A well-fed specimen is critical to prevent coral nipping.

imageCandidates.notes: Photo by Dr. Dwayne Meadows, NOAA/NMFS/OPR. Taken at Chuuk, Federated States of Micronesia, September 2006. 1792×1264 px, 700 KB. Clear specimen shot, widely used as the canonical Wikipedia infobox image for this species.
FishBase summary photo by J.E. Randall (Marshall Islands). License not explicitly stated on FishBase page. Confirmed accessible (returns JPEG binary). Good quality field photograph.
LiveAquaria product hero image. Clean composition, retailer photography typical of trade morph appearance. All rights reserved.

summary: Hardy, peaceful herbivore from Indo-Pacific reefs with vivid yellow coloring and a distinctive fox-like face. Excellent algae controller with venomous dorsal spines; reef-safe when well fed.
careNotes: The Foxface Lo is one of the hardier saltwater fish and is a great candidate for established reef aquariums with algae problems. It can be kept in a 125-gallon (473 L) or larger aquarium. The fish is generally peaceful but will become aggressive toward conspecifics and other rabbitfish — keep only one per tank unless in a very large system with a bonded pair. IMPORTANT: All dorsal and anal fin spines are venomous and can inflict a painful sting; handle only with a net or by directing the fish with aquarium tools, never with bare hands. The species is a color-changer: when stressed, sleeping, or frightened it adopts a mottled dark-brown camouflage pattern — this is normal and not a sign of illness. Reef safety depends on feeding: a well-fed specimen will typically ignore corals, but underfed fish may nip at soft corals, LPS, and zoanthids. Provide ample algae (nori clips, seaweed sheets) and consider supplementing with herbivore-formulated foods. Juveniles may school in groups around Acropora corals in the wild but adults are generally kept singly in captivity. Temperature note: FishBase habitat models give a wild range of 24.7–29 °C; aquarium trade sources commonly cite 72–78 °F (22–25.6 °C). The broader combined range (22–29 °C) is recorded here; optimal aquarium temperature is 24–27 °C.
breedingNotes: Breeding in captivity is extremely rare and not documented reliably. The species is a pelagic broadcast spawner that responds to lunar phases and tidal cues, making replication in home aquaria essentially infeasible. Wild pairs form monogamous bonds and spawn in open water. No commercial captive-breeding programs are known.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (25 cm max), temperature range (24.7–29 °C), native distribution, taxonomy (family Siganidae, order Acanthuriformes), and diet. Temperature range on FishBase (24.7–29 °C) is wider than trade sources (72–78 °F / 22–25.6 °C); broader range recorded in waterParameters with explanation in careNotes.

additional.name: Wikipedia – Siganus vulpinus
additional.notes: Used for image candidate identification (Wikimedia Commons infobox photo). Confirmed taxonomy, native range, and diet (omnivorous per Wikipedia; herbivorous per FishBase trophic level 2.7 — minor discrepancy; Wikipedia notes algae plus zooplankton; primary diet is algae so 'herbivore' used).

additional.name: Build Your Aquarium – Foxface Rabbitfish
additional.notes: Secondary cross-check of adult size, salinity, temperature, tank volume (75 gal = 284 L minimum cited; LiveAquaria's 125 gal / 473 L used as conservative figure). Confirmed peaceful temperament and solitary grouping.

fish.breedingNotes: Pelagic broadcast spawner responding to lunar and tidal cues; no reliable captive breeding documented.
